access.accessMethod: Submit CUL’s imaging and permissions request form, choosing private-study copies or the appropriate publication/commercial-use option. Item condition, deposited-item authority, quotation, and any owner approval remain subject to CUL review.
access.entityLabel: Cambridge, Selwyn College, MS 108.L.1 (S)
access.format: Item-specific JPEG or TIFF images on request

localCopies.label: Cambridge University Library ordering images page
notes: Institutional image-order and permission guidance only; it contains no target transcription or images.
CUL says it supplies a quote after reviewing the request; exact cost and coverage for MS 108.L.1 are therefore unknown.
Private-study supply and permission for publication or other reuse are distinct options; CUL’s image permission does not clear further underlying rights.
Whether deposited-item imaging additionally requires Selwyn College approval was not established.

rights.basis: CUL separates private-study image supply from publication and other reuse, requires its licence for use of CUL-supplied images, and states that its permission does not clear further underlying rights. No item-specific quote, licence, or Selwyn-owner approval for MS 108.L.1 was obtained.
rights.component: Images or copies supplied by Cambridge University Library and permission for their requested use

dateStatements.label: Current MWM date
dateStatements.value: Beginning of the fifteenth century (s. xv in)

holding.notes: Owned and cataloged under Selwyn College’s shelfmark; Cambridge University Library states that Selwyn’s medieval manuscripts are deposited at CUL and may be consulted in its Special Collections Reading Room.

entities.label: Cambridge, Selwyn College, MS 108.L.1 (S)
notes: Main collection at ff. 1r–139r: Prologue, Catholic and Pauline Epistles, Acts, and Matthew 1:1–6:13.
Paues used S as base for the Prologue, Epistles, and Matthew, but not Acts.
MWM assigns Herefordshire scribal dialect to the Prologue and Epistles; this is not automatically a place of composition.
Current MWM catalog describes one Anglicana Formata scribe; Paues had assigned ff. 86r–101v to a second hand.

next_steps: Collate all five witnesses and relevant collateral biblical manuscripts.
Consult recent scholarship on Selwyn 108.L.1 and non-Wycliffite Middle English scripture.
Keep dialect, scribal origin, composition, compilation, and physical exemplar distinct.
openQuestions.question: How many translation and compilation events produced the Southern nucleus and Midland additions, and what exact dates, places, and Latin exemplars underlie each component?

next_steps: Search Paues, Upsala, Newnham, Cambridge University Press, and Bodleian archival records.
Do not replace the explicit transcript intermediary with a direct edition-to-D transcription claim.
openQuestions.question: Who was Miss Haggitt, what did her Douce 250 transcript contain, did Paues also inspect D directly, and where does the transcript now survive?

next_steps: Obtain current palaeographical opinions and inspect both manuscripts at high resolution.
Reassess the dating intervals, direction of copying, and possibility of catalog legacy dating before normalizing one chronology.
openQuestions.question: How should the current Parker MODS date of approximately 1375–1399 and MWM’s early-fifteenth-century date for S be reconciled with Paues’s conclusion that P was copied from corrected S and her earlier-half-fifteenth-century date for P?

next_steps: Inspect the relevant Selwyn folios directly or in new high-resolution images.
Consult Ker 1977 and recent Selwyn scholarship.
openQuestions.question: How should Paues’s second hand at S ff. 86r–101v be reconciled with Ker and the MWM catalog’s one-scribe description?

next_steps: Submit an item-specific CUL Imaging Services enquiry for MS 108.L.1 to establish available coverage, condition approval, quote, supplied format, and licence terms.
Ask CUL and, if directed, library@sel.cam.ac.uk whether Selwyn-owner approval is additionally required for reproduction or publication of this deposited manuscript.
Contact Cambridge University Library and Bodleian Special Collections about C, D, and H.
Recheck institutional digital catalogs and record exact provider-object coverage before adding facsimile entities.
openQuestions.question: Are complete current public facsimiles or item-specific reproductions available for S, C, D, or H, what are their exact image rights and coverage, and does imaging deposited S require approval from Selwyn College in addition to CUL’s licence?

next_steps: Seek U-M DLPS ingest/migration documentation and CME import history.
Download and byte-compare a current serialization if U-M exposes one.
Keep copied_from bounded to the byte-compared raw package and current U-M as a qualified version_of relation.
openQuestions.question: What transfer and transformation history connects the May 2006 raw U-M package, the current public U-M item, and the normalized repository XML?

next_steps: Request U-M item-level rights provenance and effective-date documentation.
Do not treat NoC-US as a license or as a retroactive worldwide determination for legacy package bytes.
openQuestions.question: How should the legacy raw-package copyright warning and current U-M No Copyright–United States statement be reconciled for exact raw XML/TIFF files and modern provider layers?

next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or complete microfilm and reconcile historical and modern foliations.
Verify whether the current TEI endpoint ‘162va’ is intentional and audit the PIMS film’s exact coverage.
openQuestions.question: How do Paues’s Holkham Hall 672 locator beginning at f. 132, the current MS Holkham misc. 40 item at ff. 133r–162va, and the PIMS microfilm sequence correspond exactly?

next_steps: Obtain jurisdiction-specific legal review before redistribution beyond documented licenses.
Keep physical ownership, textual copyright, image rights, metadata rights, and provider contract terms separate.
openQuestions.question: What copyright or unpublished-work rules apply outside the United States to Paues’s editions, the five medieval witness texts, the Haggitt transcript, and modern catalog/image layers?

reviewNotes: Repository-to-raw derivation is supported/high, not confirmed, because semantic equivalence is exact under documented normalizations but the transformer is undocumented.
The exact raw package and conceptual current U-M item are distinct entities; no current serialization was byte-compared.
The 350-image U-M package is complete for the provider’s printed block, not the physical binding.
Parker MS 434 is the only witness with a verified complete public provider facsimile in this research.
Catalog records, MODS/TEI metadata, the PIMS checklist, and Copyright Office guidance are descriptive/support layers outside the textual chain.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a normalized copy of an exact legacy U-M package encoding Paues’s 1904 edited selection. The 1904 text revises a privately printed 1902 thesis predecessor and combines differentiated evidence from five witnesses: S for the Prologue, Epistles, and Matthew; C for Acts; P for collation; D through Haggitt’s loaned transcript for Appendix I and other readings; and H for Appendix II variants. No single witness contains the complete cataloged selection. Digital facsimile, catalog, legal-support, access, and rights layers are modeled separately from historical textual transmission.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator(s) and compiler
catalogSummary.date: Fourteenth century, probably with distinct Southern and Midland layers; first printed privately 1902; revised publication 1904; electronic encoding 2006
catalogSummary.region: England; composite Southern or South-Western and Midland or North-East Midland textual layers
catalogSummary.title: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version

catalogingSubject.label: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The exact 1904 Paues edited selection represented by the pinned repository XML: title matter, twenty-three main prose components, four Appendix I prose versions, and a zero-item Appendix II variants apparatus. It is neither one medieval work nor one complete manuscript, codex, Bible, or New Testament; Paues assembled it from five witnesses, and no single witness supplies every layer.

contentParts.label: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version (1904 edited selection)
notes: This is an edited selection assembled from five witnesses and editorial apparatus, not one medieval work or one complete New Testament.
The 350-image source package includes the whole photographed printed block, but the selected textual content occupies title images 2–4 and textual images 87–315.
contentParts.summary: The exact edited selection represented by the repository XML: title matter, Paues’s main Prologue-and-New-Testament text, Appendix I’s four Douce-tradition versions, and Appendix II’s Holkham variants; excluded front and back matter is recorded structurally but not counted as a textual item.

contentParts.label: 1904 title matter
contentParts.summary: Transcribed title page naming the edition and Anna C. Paues.

contentParts.label: Omitted publication matter
notes: Non-contiguous omitted matter is one zero-count structural wrapper; it is image-linked and page-break-linked but not part of the transcribed 27-item textual selection.
contentParts.summary: Consolidated structural record for the photographed half-title, untranscribed introduction and other front matter, and untranscribed back matter.

contentParts.label: Main text
notes: The boundary page image 00000086 carries printed p. lxxxvi before the main-text start and is not assigned to a count-bearing leaf.
contentParts.summary: Paues’s main edited text: Prologue, Catholic and Pauline Epistles, Acts, and Matthew 1:1–6:13.

contentParts.label: Appendix II: variants from Holkham Hall MS 672
notes: The apparatus has 22 P and 4 PB elements but no DIV2, DIV3, MILESTONE, or NOTE1 elements, so it does not add a twenty-eighth prose item.

dateStatements.display: Fourteenth century; probably multiple translation layers
notes: Paues’s title and analysis support a fourteenth-century tradition, but later scholarship may refine whether every component is entirely fourteenth-century.

dateStatements.display: Fourteenth century, perhaps the second half

scope.description: Assembly of the composite main collection, not composition of every translated component

dateStatements.display: Latter fourteenth century in Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39
Privately printed in May 1902 as Paues’s Upsala doctoral thesis
Revised and publicly issued by Cambridge University Press in 1904, with separate introduction and two appendices

extent.description: Twenty-seven non-overlapping prose items: twenty-three main-text components and four Appendix I alternative versions. Thirty substantive sections comprise the main container plus 23 leaves, Appendix I container plus 4 leaves, and Appendix II apparatus. The full content_parts hierarchy has 33 nodes after adding the zero-count selection root, title wrapper, and consolidated omitted-matter wrapper.

formStatements.description: All twenty-seven count-bearing textual components are prose.

scope.description: Primary form of the cataloged edited selection, determined by its twenty-seven count-bearing prose components; title, omitted wrappers, and Appendix II remain separately typed as editorial or structural matter

formStatements.description: Title, omitted wrappers, and variants apparatus are structural or editorial layers rather than additional prose works.

genres.label: Scholarly edition
notes: This classifies Paues’s 1904 editorial presentation, not the anonymous medieval collection as originally a scholarly edition.

scope.description: Modern editorial title, headings, apparatus, and omitted publication matter

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is scoped as quoted or rubric apparatus and is not a co-primary language of the twenty-seven Middle English prose components.

scope.description: Vulgate comparison readings, biblical rubrics, headings, and brief apparatus phrases

notes: Count axes are deliberately separate: 27 numbered prose items, 30 substantive sections, and 33 explicit content-part hierarchy nodes.
Witness dates, physical descriptions, holdings, image access, and image rights remain in the lineage manifest and do not become undifferentiated whole-selection properties.

No single witness carries the complete cataloged selection: S and P cover the main collection, C supplies the Acts base, D underlies Appendix I through a transcript, and H supplies Appendix II variants.
Latin is quoted or rubric language only; the primary textual language of every count-bearing component is Middle English.

next_steps: Collate all five witnesses and relevant collateral biblical manuscripts against the Vulgate tradition.
Consult recent scholarship on Selwyn College MS 108.L.1 and non-Wycliffite Middle English scripture.
Keep dialectal, scribal, compositional, and exemplar evidence distinct.
openQuestions.question: How many translation and compilation events produced the Southern nucleus and Midland additions, and what exact dates, places, and Latin exemplars underlie each component?

next_steps: Search Paues correspondence, Upsala thesis papers, Cambridge University Press archives, and Bodleian reader or reproduction records.
Do not collapse the transcript intermediary into direct edition-to-manuscript transcription without evidence.
openQuestions.question: Who was Miss Haggitt, what portion of Douce 250 did her transcript cover, did Paues also inspect the manuscript directly, and where does the transcript now survive?

next_steps: Inspect Selwyn MS 108.L.1 directly or through new high-resolution images.
Compare the relevant folios palaeographically and consult Ker 1977 and recent Selwyn scholarship.
openQuestions.question: How should Paues’s claim of a second hand at Selwyn ff. 86r–101v be reconciled with Ker and the current MWM catalog’s one-scribe description?

next_steps: Contact the four holding institutions and recheck their current digital-library catalogs.
Record exact image coverage and terms before adding any facsimile relation.
openQuestions.question: Are complete current public facsimiles or orderable reproductions available for Selwyn 108.L.1, CUL Dd.12.39, Douce 250, or Holkham misc. 40?

next_steps: Seek U-M DLPS ingest and migration documentation and compare a current downloadable serialization if one becomes available.
Retain copied_from only for the byte-compared raw package and keep the raw-to-current relation qualified as version_of.
openQuestions.question: What transfer and transformation history connects the May 2006 raw U-M package, the current public U-M presentation, and the normalized repository XML?

next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or a complete reproduction and reconcile old and modern foliations.
Preserve the current TEI locator literally until the endpoint can be verified.
openQuestions.question: How do Paues’s historical Holkham Hall 672 locators and siglum H map exactly onto the current MS Holkham misc. 40 foliation, especially the current catalog endpoint ‘162va’?

scope.description: Broad cultural origin; not a single demonstrated place of composition
Paues’s more specific South-Western characterization, with a possible Kent or South-East translator background
Midland additions to the collection, with Southern admixture
Paues’s probable North or North-East Midland source layer

notes: This describes the Selwyn scribe’s language, not the underlying translation’s place of composition.

scope.description: Scribal dialect of Selwyn College MS 108.L.1’s Prologue and Epistles

notes: Paues’s dialectal and textual analysis indicates at least a Southern nucleus and Midland additions; assigning one translator would overstate the evidence.

scope.description: Middle English translation responsibility across the twenty-seven count-bearing prose components; this does not assert one translator

notes: This is a functional cataloging responsibility for the composite collection, not an identification of a person or a claim that all components originated together.

scope.description: The medieval collection represented by the main Prologue, Epistles, Acts, and Matthew
The 1904 source edition, including selection, correction, headings, collation, Appendix I, and Appendix II
The 1902 private thesis predecessor containing the Prologue and main biblical text but not the 1904 appendices

notes: The exact extent and survival of Haggitt’s transcript remain unknown.

scope.description: At least the Douce 250 passages used or collated by Paues, mediated through the loaned transcript
2006 OCR and Level-1 TEI-in-Libraries encoding with linked page images

subjects.label: Religious women as intended readers
notes: The Prologue addresses a ‘sister’; this is audience evidence, not authorship or institutional provenance.

summaries.value: An edited Middle English scriptural collection comprising a doctrinal Prologue, the Catholic and Pauline Epistles except Philemon, Acts, and Matthew 1:1–6:13, supplemented by four alternative Catholic Epistle versions from the Douce tradition and a Holkham variants apparatus. Paues’s witnesses preserve different portions and textual states; the main text principally follows Selwyn College MS 108.L.1, with Acts from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39.

The cataloged subject is the exact Paues/U-M edited selection, not all matter in the 1904 printed volume, not the 1902 thesis as a whole, and not a blanket identity with any one medieval witness. Twenty-seven non-overlapping prose components are counted once; Appendix II is apparatus with zero additional items.

Paues distinguishes a Southern or South-Western nucleus (Prologue, Peter, James, 1 John, and the Pauline Epistles) from Midland additions, especially 2–3 John, Jude, Acts, and Matthew; the exact number, order, and geography of translation and compilation events remain unresolved.

The pinned XML contains 12,162 elements: 6 DIV1, 27 DIV2, 134 DIV3, 31 HEAD, 202 P, 350 PB, 3,843 MILESTONE, 3,018 NOTE1, 351 EPB, 3 TRAILER, and 4,150 HI elements. The main text has 23 DIV2 components, Appendix I has four, and Appendix II has none.

Middle English is primary for all twenty-seven count-bearing prose components. Modern English supplies editorial framing and apparatus; Latin occurs only in quoted Vulgate readings, rubrics, headings, and brief apparatus phrases.

titles.value: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version

notes: The U-M/CME header adds the editor to the title transcription; the 1904 title page itself gives the shorter main title.

Paues’s witnesses preserve different portions and textual states; the main text principally follows Selwyn College MS 108.L.1, with Acts from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39.
